result:
:::writing
At the edge of a quiet city, there is a bakery that opens before the sun decides to show up. The lights flicker on at 4:37 a.m., never earlier, never later, as if the building itself keeps time better than any clock. Inside, the air is warm and steady, smelling of yeast, orange peel, and something faintly nostalgic, like a memory you can’t quite place.

The baker, who never gives his name, hums while he works. It’s always the same tune, though no one has ever identified it. Some say it’s a lullaby, others insist it’s a song played at old train stations. Whatever it is, it settles into the walls, into the dough, into the bones of anyone who steps inside.

By 6:00, a small line forms. A nurse finishing her shift. A man who walks his dog too early. A teenager who claims she doesn’t like mornings but shows up anyway. They don’t talk much. They just wait, watching the fog lift off the street.

When the doors open, the bell doesn’t ring—it sighs. The first breath of fresh bread rolls out like an invitation. People leave with paper bags tucked under their arms, their faces a little softer than when they arrived.

No one knows why the bakery feels different. Maybe it’s the quiet, or the routine, or the strange, steady song. Or maybe it’s just that, for a few minutes each morning, everything tastes like it’s going to be okay.
:::

By 6:00, a small line forms. A nurse finishing her shift. A man who walks his dog too early. A teenager who claims she doesn’t like mornings but shows up anyway. They don’t talk much. They just wait, watching the fog lift off the street.

When the doors open, the bell doesn’t ring—it sighs. The first breath of fresh bread rolls out like an invitation. People leave with paper bags tucked under their arms, their faces a little softer than when they arrived.

No one knows why the bakery feels different. Maybe it’s the quiet, or the routine, or the strange, steady song. Or maybe it’s just that, for a few minutes each morning, everything tastes like it’s going to be okay.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
